title: Sessions, Detach, Reattach
subtitle: The feature that survives SSH drops and laptop reboots
summary: Detach with {key:Ctrl-B}{key:d}, reattach with `tmux attach`, name sessions with `tmux new -s name`. Sessions outlive every terminal they run in.

blocks.text: A session is the outermost tmux container — a named bag of windows that keeps running even when you walk away. Detach with {key:Ctrl-B}{key:d}: tmux closes its window, the session keeps running in the background, every shell inside stays alive. Come back hours or days later, type `tmux attach` (or `tmux a` for short), and you're back exactly where you left off.

rows: `tmux` — attach to default unnamed session, or create one
`tmux new -s work` — start a new session named *work*
`tmux attach -t work` — attach to session named *work*

blocks.text: If you SSH into a server, *immediately* run `tmux` (or `tmux attach`) before doing real work. SSH connections drop. tmux sessions don't. Anyone who has ever lost half an hour of work to a flaky network already knows why.

rows: You close it ({key:Ctrl-B}{key:&}) or session ends
Its shell exits or you kill it ({key:Ctrl-B}{key:x})

blocks.text: The classic mistake is to use just one session for everything. Once you have more than five or six windows in one session, switching becomes a chore. Split into named sessions per project — `work`, `personal`, `experiments` — and use {key:Ctrl-B}{key:s} or `tmux switch-client` to hop between them. Each session has its own window list, its own scrollback, its own state.

blocks.title: How Detach Actually Works
blocks.text:
tmux runs as a long-lived server process, separate from any terminal. When you start `tmux`, the binary spawns the server (if it isn't already running) and then connects to it as a client. Your keystrokes flow client → server → pane. The server sits in the kernel's process table, owns all the shells inside it, and outlives any individual client connection.

*Detach* simply disconnects the client. The server keeps running. The shells inside the panes have no idea anything happened — their controlling terminal is the tmux server's pseudo-terminal device, which doesn't go away. *Attach* is just "create a new client connection to the existing server."

This is why tmux sessions survive SSH drops and laptop sleeps: the SSH disconnect kills your client, but the server (running on the remote host, owned by your user account) doesn't care. Reconnect, run `tmux attach`, get a fresh client, see your work. The architecture is the entire feature.
